feedback.text: I´d would be much appreciated if this act also would protect other digital media such as videogames. Many of todays videogames are created in a way that they require the internet and servers for mutliplayer and I ofcourse find this ok it´s a choice the develoopers made. But games that also have singleplayer components, campaigns or is actually playable as a singleplayer even in a multiplayer enviroment should not need to be made unplayable even after a games servers has gone offline. I´m in no way saying that game publishers/developers should require the game to be eternally avalible but to make it playable even after it´s abandoned would be a welcome addon. The games of old often cost 50 EUR and it´s a sizeable purchase for many in the EU to pay these fees. Now todays games are going upwards of 80 EUR and is even more of a financial strain. It would be too much too ask that the publisher/developer atleast have to try to make these game hostable on private servers or that online copy protections are taken out when the time comes for the game to be shut down. Ofcourse added bonuses can be implemented that would respect the time that players have spent on the game and even make save files avalible so a characters progress could be saved but I very much see this as a smaller priority. Even when I buy a Physical disc today the entire game is most surely not on that disc. The build that gets sent out is pretty much always buggy and might not even boot up so a day one patch is applied upon the first start. This is also lost when the games servers are taken offline and so we are stuck with more actual landfill material for no real reason. I have been playing games since my Childhood and even the games in the 1980´s I can boot up on my Vintage C64 and actually play. Same goes with the PC games and console games of the pre internet era where no patches could be applied. New times calls for new meausures though and the Internet has brought us alot of joy but also alot of new problems to tackle. Today the only way you can ensure that the game you purchased is avalible to play in the distant future is also to download a pirated version of the game. This cannot be the way it´s supposed to work and adds to the dilemma. Please look into this at the same time as this Digital Fairness Act is incorporated. Digital media is here to stay and it would be so nice if my daughter could play some of the games she loved with her children or grandchildren one day as I have done with her!
